content.text: Technical feasibility for a GenAI use case is not a binary yes/no judgement — it is an assessment of which integration pattern is achievable given the current state of the organisation's infrastructure, data pipelines, tooling, and engineering skills. A use case that is technically infeasible with a Direct API integration may be highly feasible with an AI Gateway. A use case that requires an Agent Loop may be infeasible today but feasible in 6 months once a tool registry is built. The technical feasibility assessment produces a recommendation for which integration pattern to use and what infrastructure gaps must be closed before delivery.

attrs.alt: Four GenAI integration patterns: Direct API, AI Gateway, RAG, and Agent Loop

content.text: GenAI integration patterns form a complexity hierarchy. Simpler patterns are faster to build, easier to operate, and lower risk — but deliver less capability. More complex patterns require more infrastructure, more engineering skill, and more governance — but enable use cases that simpler patterns cannot support. The feasibility assessment selects the lowest-complexity pattern that meets the use case requirements.
The application calls the model API directly over HTTPS. No proxy, no gateway, no intermediary. The simplest possible integration.
— Prototyping or internal tooling with no production data, no audit requirement, and a single developer managing the API key.

— The use case handles production data, requires an audit trail, will be accessed by multiple teams, or needs rate limiting and cost control. Direct API integration in production is an anti-pattern: there is no audit log, no rate limit enforcement, no model version control, and no guardrail enforcement. One misconfigured or compromised API key gives unlimited access to the model with no visibility.
— Network connectivity from the application host to the model API endpoint; API key management (environment variable or secrets manager); HTTPS support.

A proxy layer sits between the application and the model API. The gateway handles authentication, rate limiting, audit logging, guardrail enforcement, and model routing. All production GenAI use cases should use an AI gateway.
— Any production use case; multiple teams accessing the same model; cost control is required; audit trail is required by governance or regulation.

— Centralised API key management (no per-team API keys); request/response logging with PII redaction before logging; rate limiting per team, per use case, and per user; guardrail enforcement (content filtering, prompt injection detection); model routing (route to different model versions or providers based on request type); cost tracking per use case.

— Gateway deployment (open-source options: LiteLLM, Portkey, Kong AI; enterprise options: Azure API Management, AWS API Gateway + Lambda); gateway must be in the network path between all AI-enabled applications and external model APIs.

The AI gateway is infrastructure, not a feature. It should be deployed by the CoE platform team before the first production use case goes live — not retrofitted after three teams have built their own direct integrations.

The application retrieves relevant context from a vector database (or hybrid search index) before calling the model. The retrieved context is injected into the prompt, grounding the model's response in the organisation's own data.

— The use case requires responses grounded in enterprise documents, policies, or knowledge bases that are too large to fit in a single prompt; when the model's training data does not include the organisation's proprietary information; when reducing hallucination is a priority.

— A vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector, Azure Cognitive Search); an embedding model to convert documents and queries into vector representations; a document ingestion pipeline that chunks, embeds, and indexes the source documents; ongoing re-ingestion pipeline to keep the index current when source documents change.

— Is the source document corpus in a machine-readable format (not scanned PDFs)? Is there a document management system from which the ingestion pipeline can pull updates? Is the embedding model's output vector dimension compatible with the chosen vector database?

An orchestrator (LangChain, LlamaIndex, custom loop) manages a conversation between the model and a set of registered tools. The model decides which tool to call, calls it, receives the output, and decides the next step — repeating until the task is complete.
— The task requires multi-step reasoning, data retrieval from multiple systems, or autonomous decision-making across a sequence of actions that cannot be reduced to a single prompt.

— A tool registry that defines available tools (name, description, input schema, output schema); tool implementations (wrappers around existing APIs or data systems); persistent state management (so the agent can pause and resume workflows); step-level audit logging; circuit breakers and maximum step count enforcement; human approval checkpoint support (for Human-in-Loop mode).

— Do the enterprise systems the agent needs to interact with have APIs? Is there an API gateway through which the agent's tool calls can be routed and logged? Does the team have experience building and debugging agentic systems, including handling tool failures and replanning?

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

IntegrationPattern = Literal["DirectAPI", "AIGateway", "RAG", "AgentLoop"]

@dataclass
class TechFeasibilityAssessment:
    use_case: str
    # Infrastructure readiness (1=not ready, 5=fully ready)
    api_connectivity:       int  # can the app reach the model API?
    auth_iam_maturity:      int  # is API key / OAuth management in place?
    observability_infra:    int  # logging, tracing, alerting available?
    data_pipeline_maturity: int  # can we get the right data to the model?
    # Pattern-specific readiness
    vector_db_available:    bool  # for RAG
    tool_registry_exists:   bool  # for Agent Loop
    gateway_deployed:       bool  # for AI Gateway / Agent Loop
    # Latency requirements
    max_p95_latency_ms: int  # use case SLA
    # Skills
    ml_engineers_available: int  # FTE with relevant skills

    @property
    def base_feasibility(self) -> float:
        return (
            self.api_connectivity * 0.25 +
            self.auth_iam_maturity * 0.20 +
            self.observability_infra * 0.20 +
            self.data_pipeline_maturity * 0.35
        )

    def pattern_feasibility(self, pattern: IntegrationPattern) -> float:
        base = self.base_feasibility
        if pattern == "DirectAPI":
            return base  # no additional requirements
        if pattern == "AIGateway":
            return base * (0.9 if not self.gateway_deployed else 1.0)
        if pattern == "RAG":
            rag_bonus = 0.9 if not self.vector_db_available else 1.0
            return base * rag_bonus * (0.95 if self.data_pipeline_maturity < 3 else 1.0)
        if pattern == "AgentLoop":
            agent_mult = (
                (0.85 if not self.gateway_deployed else 1.0) *
                (0.90 if not self.tool_registry_exists else 1.0) *
                (0.85 if self.ml_engineers_available < 2 else 1.0)
            )
            return base * agent_mult
        return base

    def recommended_pattern(self) -> IntegrationPattern:
        scores = {
            p: self.pattern_feasibility(p)
            for p in ("DirectAPI", "AIGateway", "RAG", "AgentLoop")
        }
        # Never recommend DirectAPI for production use cases (no audit trail)
        scores["DirectAPI"] -= 0.5
        return max(scores, key=scores.get)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"=== Tech Feasibility: {self.use_case} ==="]
        lines.append(f"Base feasibility score: {self.base_feasibility:.2f}/5.00")
        for pattern in ("DirectAPI", "AIGateway", "RAG", "AgentLoop"):
            score = self.pattern_feasibility(pattern)
            flag = " <-- RECOMMENDED" if pattern == self.recommended_pattern() else ""
            lines.append(f"  {pattern:<14}: {score:.2f}{flag}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

# FNOL triage assessment
fnol = TechFeasibilityAssessment(
    use_case="FNOL Auto-Triage",
    api_connectivity=4,
    auth_iam_maturity=3,
    observability_infra=3,
    data_pipeline_maturity=4,
    vector_db_available=False,
    tool_registry_exists=False,
    gateway_deployed=True,
    max_p95_latency_ms=2000,
    ml_engineers_available=2,
)
print(fnol.summary())

# Claims research agent assessment
research_agent = TechFeasibilityAssessment(
    use_case="Claims Research Agent",
    api_connectivity=4,
    auth_iam_maturity=3,
    observability_infra=2,
    data_pipeline_maturity=3,
    vector_db_available=True,
    tool_registry_exists=False,
    gateway_deployed=True,
    max_p95_latency_ms=30000,
    ml_engineers_available=1,
)
print()
print(research_agent.summary())
For each integration pattern, the following infrastructure components must be evaluated before the use case enters the build phase:

Each integration pattern adds latency relative to a baseline inference call. The use case latency requirement (from the business process SLA) must be validated against the pattern's typical latency profile:
Model inference time; output token count; network RTT to provider
Direct API + gateway overhead (~50–200ms); guardrail processing adds 100–500ms
Gateway + embedding model call (~100–300ms) + vector search (~50–200ms) + inference on larger context
Gateway × N steps + tool call latency × N steps; context grows each step, increasing inference time
Scales linearly with step count; tool failures trigger replanning, doubling worst-case time

For synchronous use cases where a user waits for a response (customer-facing chatbot, real-time document assistant), the P95 latency must be within the user experience threshold — typically < 5–8 seconds for a full response. For asynchronous use cases where the user receives a notification when the task completes (document processing batch, agent research task), latency requirements are much looser and Agent Loop patterns become viable.
Technical infrastructure is one dimension of feasibility; engineering skills are another. The skills required differ significantly by pattern:
1 backend engineer with HTTPS API experience; prompt engineering basics
Team has no experience with LLM API calls
1 platform engineer for gateway setup; backend engineer for integration
No platform/DevOps capacity; gateway setup attempted by application engineers without platform support
1 ML engineer with embeddings and vector search experience; data engineer for ingestion pipeline; backend engineer for integration
No experience with chunking strategy or vector search tuning; no data engineering capacity for ingestion pipeline
1–2 ML engineers with agent framework experience; tool implementation engineers (one per integrated system); platform engineer for state management
Team has never built an agentic system; tool implementations not owned by the AI team; no experience debugging multi-step reasoning failures

Agent Loop patterns are frequently selected during ideation for their capability without understanding the engineering complexity. A use case that could be implemented as a well-designed RAG pattern should not be built as an Agent Loop if the team lacks agentic engineering experience — the failure rate in the build phase is significantly higher and the debugging surface is much larger.
Selecting Agent Loop to avoid prompt engineering work

— A well-engineered RAG prompt that retrieves the right context can often answer a complex question with a single inference call. Teams that struggle with prompt engineering sometimes escalate to an Agent Loop because 'the agent will figure it out'. The agent compounds the prompt engineering problem across multiple steps rather than solving it.
RAG without a document ingestion pipeline plan

— Indexing a corpus of 1,000 documents once is straightforward. Keeping that index current as documents are updated, deleted, or added — without a scheduled ingestion pipeline — is not. A stale RAG index produces outdated responses that erode user trust faster than a model with no RAG at all.

— Enterprise APIs are designed for human-authored requests with validation, error handling, and known input formats. Wrapping an existing API as an agent tool often reveals that the API's error responses are not machine-readable, its input validation is too strict for LLM-generated parameters, or its authentication requires interactive flows that agents cannot complete. Budget 2–5x the estimated tool implementation time for enterprise API integration.

— If the use case involves data that must remain within a specific geographic region (EU data under GDPR, UK financial data under FCA rules), the model API endpoint must be in that region. Not all providers offer all model tiers in all regions. Check regional availability before committing to a model tier.
Use these questions in order to narrow the integration pattern before performing the full feasibility scoring. Each question eliminates one or more patterns:
Is this a prototype or production use case?
If prototype, Direct API is acceptable. If production, skip Direct API entirely.
Does the use case require enterprise data not in the model's training set?
If yes, eliminate Direct API and AI Gateway as stand-alone patterns — RAG or Agent Loop (with RAG capability) is required.
Is the task completable in a single context window?

If the user's question plus retrieved context fits in one prompt, RAG is sufficient. If the task requires multi-step action across multiple systems — reading from System A, computing, writing to System B — Agent Loop is required.
What is the P95 latency requirement?
If < 5s, Agent Loop with more than 3 steps is infeasible. If < 2s, RAG with slow embedding models is infeasible.
Is a tool registry available or can one be built within the delivery timeline?
If no tool registry exists and building one is not in scope, Agent Loop is infeasible regardless of other factors.

A general insurance carrier is assessing technical feasibility for three distinct AI use cases within its claims processing platform. Each maps to a different integration pattern based on its requirements.
Use Case A — Coverage Verification Assistant

An internal adjuster tool that answers 'does this policy cover this claim type?' from the policy wordings library. The policy corpus is 4,000 PDF documents (updated quarterly); the adjuster needs a response within 4 seconds.
The question is answerable from a single retrieval + inference call — no multi-step reasoning needed.
The policy corpus fits in a vector index; a quarterly re-ingestion pipeline is straightforward.
P95 < 4s is achievable with RAG: embedding query (~150ms) + vector search (~100ms) + inference on 2K-token context (~2.5s).
— PDFs are scanned images, not text-selectable. PDF OCR pre-processing pipeline must be built before ingestion. Estimated 3-week infrastructure gap.

Automatic classification of new claims by loss type (water damage, fire, theft, liability, other) from the free-text description submitted via the web portal. The API must respond within 800ms so the portal can present the right form to the claimant in real time.
Pattern selected: AI Gateway (no RAG, no agent)
Single-call classification — a well-engineered system prompt with few-shot examples is sufficient for high accuracy.
800ms P95 is achievable with a fast model tier (GPT-4o-mini, Haiku) via gateway: inference ~300–500ms + gateway overhead ~80ms.
RAG adds 250ms+ and provides no accuracy improvement for a classification task with a fixed taxonomy.
— AI Gateway must be deployed before build begins (planned for Q2). The gateway team has capacity to deliver in 4 weeks.
Use Case C — Claims Research Agent

An agent that, given a claim number, autonomously retrieves the claim file, the insured's policy documents, comparable settled claims, and relevant case law summaries, then drafts a reserve recommendation. Adjusters review the draft, which may take 30–60 seconds to produce.
Task requires action across four distinct systems (claims management system, policy document store, historical claims database, legal knowledge base) — a single prompt cannot span all four.
60-second latency tolerance accommodates 6–10 agent steps with tool calls.

— Claims management system API returns data in COBOL copybook format, not JSON. Tool implementation requires a parsing library and data mapping layer (estimated 4 weeks). Legal knowledge base has no API — must build a RAG index over it first (estimated 6 weeks). The team has 1 ML engineer with agent framework experience; hiring 1 additional is required.

— Deliver Use Cases B (3 months) and A (4 months) first to establish the AI Gateway and RAG infrastructure. Use Case C can then build on that infrastructure base, with the tool registry as the only net-new component.
For each identified infrastructure gap, document it in a feasibility gap log that becomes an input to the project plan:
PDF OCR pre-processing pipeline not built
AI Gateway not deployed in production
Claims system API returns COBOL format
Legal knowledge base has no API
P1 — must build RAG index first
Only 1 ML engineer with agent experience
P2 — hire or upskill parallel to infrastructure work

Technical feasibility to build and technical readiness to operate in production are different questions. The following operational requirements must be confirmed before go-live — not just before build:
Monitoring and alerting for model API latency and error rate
Required — agents can consume unexpectedly high tokens if they loop
Model version pinning and rollback procedure
Required — stale index degrades quality silently
Required if agent uses RAG tools
Maximum step count enforcement with fallback
Required — prevents infinite loops; must return graceful error to caller
Tool failure handling and retry policy
Required per tool — idempotency check before retry to prevent duplicate writes
Required for Human-in-Loop and Human-on-Loop modes
Step-level logs; must be queryable by claim/session ID

Operational readiness gaps identified after build begins are the most expensive to resolve. The feasibility assessment should include an explicit operational readiness sign-off from the platform and SRE teams, not just engineering feasibility from the build team.

Technical feasibility assessment selects the lowest-complexity integration pattern that meets the use case requirements. Simpler patterns (Direct API, AI Gateway) are faster, cheaper, and lower risk; more complex patterns (RAG, Agent Loop) enable more capable use cases but require more infrastructure and skills.
Direct API integration is appropriate only for internal prototyping. All production use cases require an AI Gateway to provide audit logging, rate limiting, guardrail enforcement, and cost tracking.
RAG requires a vector database, an embedding model, and a document ingestion pipeline that keeps the index current. Stale indices produce worse user experience than no RAG at all.

Agent Loop patterns require a tool registry, tool implementations for each enterprise system the agent will interact with, persistent state management, and step-level audit logging. The skills bar is significantly higher than for other patterns.

Latency requirements constrain pattern selection. Synchronous user-facing use cases (P95 < 5–8s) generally cannot use Agent Loop patterns with more than 3–4 steps. Asynchronous use cases have much looser latency requirements.

Infrastructure gaps found during feasibility assessment should be logged with owner and resolution timeline. Gaps in AI Gateway, vector DB, and tool registry are almost always on the critical path and must be resolved before the first use case enters the build phase.
